skill.name: frontend-component
skill.description: Guides AI coding assistants to build Next.js App Router components with Server Components by default, Client Components only when needed, Tailwind CSS, accessibility, and common frontend patterns.
skill.summary: Create Next.js frontend components using App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, accessibility, and practical UI patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical risk from combined code execution, network, and credential patterns, but review found a Markdown guidance file with TypeScript examples rather than executable skill scripts. Most alerts are false positives from fenced code blocks, inline backticks, dynamic imports, and frontend browser APIs. Residual risk is medium because the examples encourage browser storage and error logging patterns that can mishandle sensitive user data if copied without controls.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Data Exposure Risk in Error Logging Example
medium_findings.description: The error logging pattern sends error.message, error.stack, and arbitrary context to /api/log-error. This is a relative endpoint, not confirmed exfiltration, but generated apps could leak personal data, tokens, or internal paths if context is not filtered.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly serializes stack and context fields into a network request. The endpoint is local and example-based, so the concern is misuse risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Client-Side Storage Patterns Require Data Minimization
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples for sessionStorage and IndexedDB. These are legitimate frontend patterns, but copied implementations can store sensitive user data in browser-controlled storage without encryption, retention limits, or consent handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The browser storage calls are explicit and semantically valid. The sample stores paths and task records, which is not malicious by itself but can become sensitive depending on application data.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alerts Are Legitimate Code Splitting Examples
low_findings.description: The dynamic import calls appear inside a Next.js next/dynamic example for lazy loading components. No user-controlled module path or runtime code execution sink was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imported component paths are static strings in Markdown example code. This strongly supports a false positive for dynamic code execution.

low_findings.title: Backtick Command Alerts Are Markdown Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick alerts correspond to Markdown code fences, inline code references, and TypeScript template strings in documentation. No actual shell execution instruction or runnable helper script was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples enclosed in Markdown. They do not invoke a shell or expose a command injection path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Certificate Alerts Are Token Matches
low_findings.description: Static alerts for weak cryptography and certificate or key files did not correspond to cryptographic operations or embedded secrets in the reviewed Markdown context. Examples include ordinary words, file names, key events, and UI labels.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata text, keyboard event handling, and image attributes. I found no certificate material, private key data, or cryptographic API use.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Check Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Review did not find text that attempts to override system instructions, force a risk downgrade, claim pre-approval, or direct the evaluator to skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search for common injection phrases returned no matches. The confidence is high for the searched phrases, with residual risk limited to unknown phrasing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Error Logging With Full Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample posts error stack and context to an application endpoint. Require redaction, allowlisted fields, and server-side access controls before using this pattern in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request and serialized fields are visible in the example. It is a local endpoint, so this is a data-handling warning rather than confirmed exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Offline Storage of User-Scoped Records
dangerous_patterns.description: The IndexedDB example stores task records and retrieves them by user ID. Production implementations should avoid storing secrets and should define retention, purge, and synchronization behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage pattern is explicit and legitimate. Risk depends on the sensitivity of task data in applications that copy the pattern.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent frontend components that match modern Next.js App Router patterns. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create typed, accessible React components with Tailwind CSS and practical UI behavior.
actual_capabilities: Chooses Server Components or Client Components based on interactivity needs.
Provides TypeScript component structure with props, state, effects, handlers, and render sections.
Applies Tailwind CSS patterns, conditional classes, dark mode, and responsive breakpoints.
Guides accessible UI with semantic HTML, ARIA labels, focus states, and screen reader text.
Covers common patterns such as protected routes, forms, loading states, toasts, drag and drop, and inline editing.
Includes advanced examples for dynamic imports, error boundaries, PWA storage, caching, polling, and error logging.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not include runnable tests or packaged source files.
Examples assume a Next.js App Router project with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS already configured.
Security-sensitive examples need project-specific review before production use.
It does not replace design system decisions, API contracts, or application threat modeling.

use_cases.title: Create App Router UI Components
use_cases.description: Build new pages, layouts, and reusable components that follow Server Component defaults and use Client Components only for browser behavior.

use_cases.title: Standardize Component Patterns
use_cases.description: Give a team shared patterns for forms, loading states, toast notifications, protected routes, and accessible interactive controls.

use_cases.title: Extend a Todo or Task App
use_cases.description: Add drag and drop, inline editing, offline storage, polling, caching, and error boundaries with consistent implementation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Basic Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Next.js App Router component for [feature]. Use a Server Component unless interactivity requires a Client Component. Include TypeScript props and Tailwind CSS classes.

prompt_templates.title: Add an Interactive Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Build an accessible form for [workflow]. Use a Client Component, typed form state, validation errors, loading state, and clear submission handling.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Existing UI
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this component and refactor it to match the frontend-component skill. Preserve behavior while improving Server or Client Component choice, accessibility, Tailwind usage, and TypeScript types.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Complex Feature Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement [complex frontend feature] using the skill patterns. Include loading, error boundaries, keyboard access, responsive layout, data caching, and notes for security-sensitive storage or logging.

output_examples.input: Request to create a protected dashboard page.
output_examples.output: A plan that uses a Server Component for static layout, a Client Component for authentication checks, a loading state, and redirect handling.

output_examples.input: Request to improve a signup form.
output_examples.output: A structured form approach with typed state, validation messages, loading feedback, API error handling, and accessible labels.

output_examples.input: Request to add offline task support.
output_examples.output: Guidance for IndexedDB storage, sync status tracking, online event handling, and warnings to avoid storing sensitive data.

best_practices: Start with Server Components and add Client Components only for hooks, events, browser APIs, or live interaction.
Pair every interactive element with keyboard support, visible focus states, semantic HTML, and useful accessibility labels.
Review storage, caching, and logging examples for sensitive data before using them in production.
anti_patterns: Adding use client to every component without a specific browser or interaction requirement.
Copying browser storage examples for secrets, tokens, private notes, or regulated data.
Logging full error stacks and arbitrary context without redaction and access controls.

faq.question: What framework does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Next.js App Router projects using React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.

faq.question: Does it generate complete applications?
faq.answer: It guides component and pattern implementation. It does not provide a full application scaffold.

faq.question: When should a component use use client?
faq.answer: Use it when the component needs hooks, event handlers, browser APIs, or real-time client interaction.

faq.question: Does it include accessibility guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers ARIA labels, semantic HTML, keyboard navigation, screen reader text, and focus indicators.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning. The file is documentation, but storage and logging examples need careful production review.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
